source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 11.1 The boot process (pp. 194-197)

questions.q: What does the BIOS do at the very start of the boot process?
options: Runs the POST to detect/test/initialise hardware, then loads the MBR
questions.why: The BIOS performs the POST (power on self-test) then loads the MBR.

questions.q: What is the MBR, and what does it do?
options: The first 512 bytes of the boot disk; it finds the boot device and loads GRUB2 into memory
questions.why: The Master Boot Record is the first 512 bytes of the boot disk; it loads the GRUB2 bootloader and hands over control (the next 64 bytes hold the partition table).

questions.q: Which GRUB2 file should you EDIT to change menu settings, and which is generated (and must not be edited directly)?
questions.why: You set options in /etc/default/grub; grub2-mkconfig uses them to generate /boot/grub2/grub.cfg, which you should not edit directly.

questions.q: Regenerate the GRUB2 configuration file after editing /etc/default/grub.
What does GRUB2 load from /boot, and what does it do with the initramfs?
options: It loads the compressed kernel (vmlinuz) into memory and extracts the initramfs into a tmpfs in memory
It loads systemd and skips the kernel
questions.why: GRUB2 finds vmlinuz in /boot, loads the kernel, and extracts the initramfs image into a temporary tmpfs in memory.

questions.q: After the kernel loads, which process does it start, and with what PID?
questions.why: The kernel starts systemd as PID 1, the parent of all system processes.

questions.q: How does systemd decide what state to bring the system into?
options: By reading the target that /etc/systemd/system/default.target links to (e.g. multi-user.target)
By scanning /boot for a runlevel file
questions.why: systemd follows the default.target symlink to find the default target, which defines the services to start.
